champion.narrative: Jenna Bruckman (Material Planning Manager) is the only engaged buyer contact and shows developing champion characteristics, but her influence is limited and engagement has gone cold. On the 2025-11-05 call, she was responsive, articulated pain clearly, expressed interest in capabilities not available in current systems ('I haven't seen that in any of the other software, so I like that' re: supplier risk), and requested next steps: 'Send an NDA so we can screen share... I'd love to see, like, a demo of how it works... I'd love to just walk you through, like, what the buyers do so you can see what our pain points are.' She also explicitly stated she would need to escalate to her boss for budget approval ('I know my boss is gonna ask, you know, how much does it cost'), indicating she is aware of the decision hierarchy and willing to advocate upward. However, she has NOT: (a) introduced Zycus to her boss, the Economic Buyer, Procurement leadership (Chris Klump, Director of Procurement), IT (for Oracle integration), or Finance (Brad Berelsman, CFO); (b) driven internal urgency (deal has been dark 106 days); (c) provided the promised NDA or arranged the detailed demo walkthrough; or (d) re-engaged after the aborted 2026-01-15 call. Three subsequent discovery sessions were scheduled but either not recorded or lasted <6 minutes and were rescheduled, signaling waning engagement or competing priorities. She is a willing operational advocate (developing champion) but lacks power, has not multi-threaded Zycus into the organization, and has gone silent for 106 days. Status: developing, now at risk of fading. The gap: no senior-level champion, no internal advocacy evidence (no introductions, no shared internal docs, no pushing against inertia), and engagement has cooled significantly.

identify_pain.narrative: Jenna Bruckman (Material Planning Manager, the pain owner) articulated urgent, high-frequency operational pain on the 2025-11-05 call. Primary pain: manual handling of exception messages in Oracle SCP for orders to expedite/de-expedite across 2,200 active SKUs. She stated: 'exception messages, we would love all those to be automated because they move around like, that is a big chunk of work right there.' The production schedule freezes only one day ahead (at 4:00 AM daily), causing constant manual adjustments: 'we only freeze the current day's production schedule... until, like, 04:00 today, tomorrow's production schedule then is frozen, and even then it's still, like, a slushy freeze... it constantly changes.' This generates daily workload for planners and buyers. Secondary pain: three senior buyers on the new product team are overloaded with manual RFX creation via email and Excel, taking approximately 40 days per RFX cycle ('We use emails and Excel spreadsheets... they could definitely use a break'). Third pain: Oracle iProcurement is universally disliked ('everybody hates it'). Fourth pain: lack of supplier risk tracking capability in current systems (Jenna responded positively to Zycus risk module: 'I haven't seen that in any of the other software, so I like that'). Pain is confirmed, operational, high-frequency, and owned by Jenna's procurement and planning teams. However, the pain has not been escalated to or validated by senior leadership (no EB/CFO engagement), and no quantified financial impact (cost of manual work, error rate, cycle time savings) has been captured. Pain is real but not yet a funded, executive-sponsored initiative.

decision_criteria.narrative: On the 2025-11-05 call, Jenna outlined several operational needs that imply evaluation criteria: (1) automation of exception messages (orders to expedite/de-expedite) in Oracle SCP, (2) automation of PO releases for repetitive/obvious orders, (3) integration with the relevant stakeholder r12 (two-way data flows for PO creation, approvals, GL postings), (4) supplier risk tracking (not available in current systems; Jenna responded positively: 'I haven't seen that in any of the other software, so I like that'), (5) RFX process improvement (replace email/Excel with template-driven, faster cycle), and (6) ease of use ('everybody hates' Oracle iProcurement). She also asked 'Would Zycus be something that adds on top of Oracle, or would it be something that reflects Oracle software?', indicating ERP add-on architecture is a criterion. However, these are inferred from a single discovery call. There is no evidence of a formal RFP, BRD (Business Requirements Document), use-case validation, or a written evaluation scorecard. The Salesforce fields 'Business_Requirements__c', 'Top_Challenges_Priorities__c', 'Decision_Criteria' (Q3 synthesis map) are not populated. No subsequent calls captured structured criteria discussion, workshops, or technical fit sessions. The criteria are directionally clear (automation, integration, supplier risk, ease-of-use, RFX speed) but remain unconfirmed, unweighted, and not formalized into a decision framework. The gap: no written BRD, no multi-stakeholder criteria alignment, no scoring model.

ai_fit_signal.summary: No AIS (AI Interest Score) data in Salesforce (AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, AIS_Why__c all null). However, Jenna's 2025-11-05 call signals AI Curious to AI Hungry: she repeatedly emphasized desire for automation ('we would love all those to be automated,' 'if there's some way to automate some of the PO releases,' 'I feel like software could make this much easier'), and she responded positively when Bailey briefly mentioned machine learning for PO creation ('it's machine learning... it will learn it'). Her pain (manual exception messages, repetitive PO work, RFX overload) is exactly the use case AI/Merlin solves. No resistance to AI detected; language suggests openness to intelligent automation. Tier: AI Curious, trending toward AI Hungry given operational pain severity and automation appetite. However, this read is based on one 8-month-old call; buyer's current AI stance is unconfirmed due to 106-day silence.

best_practice_check.summary: This deal exhibits multiple high-severity gaps that block a credible path to close by 2026-10-08 (104 days). Most critical: (1) no Economic Buyer engaged after 234 days in Qualified — the $150k spend authority is unmapped and no commercial conversation has occurred; (2) single-threaded through an operational evaluator (Jenna, Material Planning Manager) with no IT, Finance, Legal, or Procurement leadership involvement; (3) deal has been dark 106 days with no buyer re-engagement; (4) no quantified ROI to justify the investment; (5) zero competitive intelligence — unknown whether sole-sourced or in a multi-vendor bake-off; (6) no demo delivered, no NDA status confirmed, no technical validation completed despite 8+ months since creation. Best practices to raise win probability NOW: (a) Secure executive sponsorship by engaging the CFO (Brad Berelsman) or Supply Chain Chief (Aaron Bores) with a quantified ROI model tied to their cost reduction or supply chain resilience mandate; (b) Multi-thread aggressively: bring IT (Jennifer Chung, Director IT Service Operations) into Oracle integration scoping, Finance into payback analysis, and Procurement leadership (Chris Klump, Director of Procurement) into strategic alignment; (c) Create urgency by anchoring automation to a funded 2026 initiative (ERP modernization, cost takeout, supply chain transformation); (d) Deliver the long-overdue detailed demo with Jenna walking through her buyer workflow to prove fit and re-ignite momentum.

competitive_position.summary: No competitive intelligence gathered. Current stack is Oracle r12 ERP and Oracle iProcurement (universally disliked per Jenna: 'everybody hates it'), plus rapid ratings for supplier risk. No other source-to-pay vendor has been named by the buyer or captured in Salesforce. It is unknown whether Vitamix is sole-sourcing Zycus, running a silent multi-vendor evaluation, or considering 'do nothing' as the alternative. Standard competitors in this space (SAP Ariba, Coupa, Jaggaer, GEP, Ivalua) are likely but unconfirmed. The single strongest current threat is INERTIA / DO NOTHING: the deal has been dark 106 days, no Economic Buyer engaged, no quantified ROI, and Oracle iProcurement (despite being disliked) is the incumbent. Without urgency, executive sponsorship, or a competitive forcing function, the status quo wins by default.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Zycus has strong AI/Merlin positioning opportunity but has NOT leveraged it. The product scope includes 'Merlin' and 'Merlin Intake' (AI-powered agentic procurement modules), and Jenna's primary pain — manual exception message handling and PO release automation — is a high-fit use case for AI/ML-driven decisioning (the exact problem Merlin solves: automate routine, repetitive procurement actions). However, across the two transcribed calls (2025-11-05, 2026-01-15), Bailey did NOT explicitly position Merlin or frame the automation as AI-powered. On the 2025-11-05 call, Bailey mentioned 'AP Smartdesk' (AI-powered PO creation from email) and 'machine learning' once in passing ('it's machine learning. So the longer it sees this PO and gets touched on, it will learn it'), but did not lead with Merlin as the hero narrative or tie it to Jenna's stated pain. The AI narrative is under-positioned. With no AIS (AI Interest Score) data populated in Salesforce, it is unclear whether Vitamix is AI Hungry, AI Curious, or AI Resistant, but Jenna's enthusiasm for automation ('we would love all those to be automated') suggests appetite. Immediate move: re-engage with a Merlin-led demo showcasing AI-driven exception message triage and PO release automation, and tie it to industry proof points (e.g. 'Merlin reduced manual PO work by 60% for ').

customer_expectations_fit.summary: On the initial discovery call (2025-11-05), Jenna expressed interest in several Zycus capabilities that address her pain points: automation of exception messages and PO releases, supplier risk tracking (not available in current systems), faster RFX cycle (vs. 40-day email/Excel process), Oracle ERP integration (confirmed feasible by Bailey), and improved ease-of-use (vs. 'everybody hates' Oracle iProcurement). Bailey positioned Zycus as an add-on to Oracle r12 with two-way integration, which aligned with Jenna's question about architecture. However, one capability Jenna mentioned — direct manufacturing scheduling automation (freezing production schedules, managing SKU changes) — is outside Zycus's scope; Bailey explicitly stated 'that's not where we would kind of come in... I really don't know what could help with that. Frankly.' Overall fit appears strong on procurement/S2C automation (the core ask), but gaps remain: (1) no multi-stakeholder validation (IT, Finance, Procurement leadership have not confirmed fit), (2) no formal BRD or scoring, (3) no technical integration deep-dive completed, (4) direct scheduling is explicitly out-of-scope. Fit is directionally positive but unconfirmed beyond Jenna's initial reaction.
